key_insight: A feedforward neural network can classify the first six prime two-component links from the writhe density matrix with very high accuracy, showing that this geometric representation captures topology-relevant features.
engineering_relevance: This provides a fast, data-driven alternative to expensive exact topological calculations for polymer, DNA, and protein-like link classification problems.
method: Compute a writhe density matrix from thermally equilibrated link configurations.
Train a feedforward neural network on matrix features to predict link topology.
Test robustness across temperatures, component lengths, and added Gaussian noise.
evidence_signal: The model achieves 97% accuracy on thermally equilibrated configurations and remains strong across temperatures and link lengths, but performance drops sharply when topology-altering Gaussian noise is added.
watch_item: The approach is sensitive to topology-changing noise, so real-world deployment may require careful control of measurement noise and feature preprocessing.
paper_title: Writhe-Based Polymer Link Classification Using Machine Learning
